startScreenOptions.title: AN53.1-AN53.4 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for AN53.1-AN53.4.

interactions.label: Based on the clinical scenario, why did the obstetrician decide on an emergency

params.question: Based on the clinical scenario, why did the obstetrician decide on an emergency caesarean section?

answers.text: The android pelvic shape would obstruct vaginal delivery

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The narrow subpubic angle (70°), prominent ischial spines, and straight sacrum indicated an android pelvis — the male-type shape — which restricts the birth canal and is likely to obstruct vaginal delivery.

answers.text: The foetus was in a transverse lie

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: A transverse lie is a valid indication for caesarean section, but the narration does not mention foetal malpresentation. The decision was based entirely on the bony pelvis assessment findings.

answers.text: The patient had pre-eclampsia requiring immediate delivery

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Pre-eclampsia can necessitate emergency delivery, but the scenario described pelvic assessment findings, not hypertensive disease. The obstruction here was mechanical, not medical.

answers.text: The membranes had ruptured more than 24 hours earlier

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Prolonged rupture of membranes is a valid indication for intervention, but was not mentioned. The obstetrician's decision was based specifically on the bony pelvis assessment showing android features.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the clinical scenario: the narrowed subpubic angle, prominent ischial spines, and straight sacrum all pointed to an android pelvis that would obstruct vaginal delivery.
Well done. Recognising that an android pelvis mechanically obstructs the birth canal is key to understanding why pelvic assessment matters in obstetrics.

interactions.label: How is the obstetric conjugate of the pelvic inlet estimated clinically?

params.question: How is the obstetric conjugate of the pelvic inlet estimated clinically?

answers.text: Direct measurement using a pelvimeter during vaginal examination

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The obstetric conjugate cannot be measured directly because it lies between the sacral promontory and the closest point on the posterior surface of the pubic symphysis, which is inaccessible to instruments.

This reverses the relationship. The true conjugate (11 cm) is actually larger than the obstetric conjugate (10.5 cm). The estimation works by subtracting from the diagonal conjugate, not adding to the true conjugate.

Correct. The diagonal conjugate (12.5 cm) is measured per vaginam from the sacral promontory to the lower border of the pubic symphysis. Subtracting 1.5 cm gives the obstetric conjugate (10.5 cm), the smallest and most clinically important anteroposterior diameter.

answers.text: Ultrasound measurement of the anteroposterior pelvic inlet diameter

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While ultrasound can visualise pelvic structures, the obstetric conjugate is classically estimated by subtracting 1.5 cm from the clinically measured diagonal conjugate — a bedside method requiring no imaging.

overallFeedback.feedback: The obstetric conjugate (10.5 cm) is estimated by subtracting 1.5 cm from the diagonal conjugate (12.5 cm), which is the only anteroposterior diameter that can be measured per vaginam.

Excellent. The obstetric conjugate is the narrowest anteroposterior diameter the foetal head must cross — knowing how to estimate it from the diagonal conjugate is essential for clinical pelvic assessment.

interactions.label: Sacralisation of the fifth lumbar vertebra is clinically significant because it:

params.question: Sacralisation of the fifth lumbar vertebra is clinically significant because it:

answers.text: Results in six mobile lumbar vertebrae visible on imaging

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This describes lumbarisation (separation of S1), which gives six mobile lumbar segments. Sacralisation is the opposite — L5 fuses with the sacrum, leaving only four mobile lumbar vertebrae.

answers.text: Complicates vertebral level counting before epidural anaesthesia

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. When L5 fuses with the sacrum, only four mobile lumbar vertebrae remain. This makes it difficult to correctly identify the intervertebral space for epidural needle placement — a critical concern in obstetric anaesthesia.

answers.text: Increases the anteroposterior diameter of the pelvic inlet

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Sacralisation does not alter pelvic inlet diameters. It involves fusion of L5 with the sacrum, affecting vertebral counting and spinal mobility, not the dimensions of the bony pelvis.

answers.text: Causes bilateral widening of the greater sciatic notch

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The greater sciatic notch width is determined by the shape of the ilium and is a feature used in biological sex determination. It is not a consequence of sacralisation, which primarily causes low back pain and vertebral numbering errors.

overallFeedback.feedback: Sacralisation fuses L5 with the sacrum, leaving only four mobile lumbar vertebrae. This complicates vertebral level counting, which is critical before epidural anaesthesia.
Well done. Sacralisation affects about 10% of the Indian population. Recognising it on imaging prevents vertebral level errors during procedures like epidural anaesthesia.
